name: statistical-analysis
description: Statistical analysis toolkit. Hypothesis tests (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square), regression, correlation, Bayesian stats, power analysis, assumption checks, APA reporting, for academic research.
capabilities: Conduct hypothesis tests including t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square, and non-parametric alternatives
Perform regression analysis with linear, multiple, and logistic regression models
Calculate effect sizes and conduct power analyses for study planning
Check statistical assumptions with automated diagnostic tools
Apply Bayesian methods including Bayesian t-tests and regression with Bayes Factors
Generate APA-style statistical reports with proper formatting
author: davila7
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation and guidance skill for statistical analysis. All 191 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the scanner detected patterns in MARKDOWN documentation that it misinterpreted as security issues. The only Python file (scripts/assumption_checks.py) is legitimate statistical library code with no network access, no external command execution, and no suspicious patterns.
valueStatement: Statistical analysis skills are essential for research but require proper methodology. This toolkit provides comprehensive guidance on hypothesis testing, regression, Bayesian methods, and APA reporting to help researchers conduct rigorous, reproducible analyses.
limitations: Does not process user data files directly; users must provide their own datasets
Does not include data cleaning or preprocessing utilities
Does not perform machine learning or predictive modeling
Users need to install Python packages like scipy, statsmodels, pingouin, and pymc

useCases.title: Academic research analysis
useCases.description: Design studies, run hypothesis tests, and prepare APA-formatted results for publication.

useCases.title: Statistical hypothesis testing
useCases.description: Select appropriate tests, check assumptions, and interpret results with effect sizes and confidence intervals.

useCases.title: Thesis and dissertation statistics
useCases.description: Conduct power analyses, perform comprehensive assumption checks, and report findings correctly.

promptTemplates.title: Compare two groups
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me compare two groups using a t-test. I have scores for group A and group B. Check assumptions first, run the analysis, calculate Cohen's d, and report in APA format.

promptTemplates.title: Select statistical test
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to analyze the relationship between [variable type] and [variable type]. My data has [n] observations and [describe distribution]. What test should I use and how do I run it?

promptTemplates.title: Run power analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me conduct an a priori power analysis for my study. I expect an effect size of [d], want 80% power, and will use alpha = 0.05. What sample size do I need?

promptTemplates.title: Bayesian analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Run a Bayesian t-test comparing my two groups. Use weakly informative priors. Report the Bayes Factor, posterior mean difference with 95% credible interval, and probability that one group is higher.

bestPractices: Always check statistical assumptions before interpreting results and report findings
Report effect sizes with confidence intervals, not just p-values
Pre-register your analysis plan to distinguish confirmatory from exploratory findings
antiPatterns: Do not run multiple tests until finding a significant result (p-hacking)
Do not confuse statistical significance with practical importance
Do not omit effect sizes or non-significant findings from your report

faq.question: What sample size do I need for my study?
faq.answer: Conduct an a priori power analysis specifying expected effect size, alpha level (typically 0.05), and desired power (typically 0.80).

faq.question: How do I check if my data meets normality assumptions?
faq.answer: Use Shapiro-Wilk test for small samples (n < 50), visual Q-Q plots for all sample sizes, and examine histograms.

faq.question: When should I use non-parametric tests instead of t-tests?
faq.answer: Use non-parametric tests when normality is severely violated and sample size is small, or when data is ordinal.

faq.question: How do I handle multiple comparisons in my analysis?
faq.answer: Apply corrections like Bonferroni for strict control or Tukey's HSD for post-hoc ANOVA comparisons.

faq.question: What effect size should I report for different tests?
faq.answer: Report Cohen's d for t-tests, eta-squared for ANOVA, r for correlations, and R-squared for regression.

faq.question: When should I use Bayesian instead of frequentist methods?
faq.answer: Use Bayesian methods when you have prior information, want direct probability statements, or have small samples.
